title: Raj Kapoor

infobox.value: Ranbir Raj Kapoor  (1924-12-14)14 December 1924  [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"), [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India")
Ranbir Raj Kapoor *Showman of Bollywood* *[Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin") of India*
Five, including [Randhir](/wiki/Randhir_Kapoor "Randhir Kapoor"), [Rishi](/wiki/Rishi_Kapoor "Rishi Kapoor"), and [Rajiv](/wiki/Rajiv_Kapoor "Rajiv Kapoor")[[1]](#cite_note-1)
[Full list](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Raj_Kapoor "List of awards and nominations received by Raj Kapoor")
* [Padma Bhushan](/wiki/Padma_Bhushan "Padma Bhushan") (1971) * [Dadasaheb Phalke Award](/wiki/Dadasaheb_Phalke_Award "Dadasaheb Phalke Award") (1988)

(pronounced [[raːdʒ kəˈpuːɾ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Hindi_and_Urdu "Help:IPA/Hindi and Urdu")) (14 December 1924 – 2 June 1988[[2]](#cite_note-2)) was an Indian actor, film director, and producer, who worked in [Hindi cinema](/wiki/Hindi_cinema "Hindi cinema").[[3]](#cite_note-3) He is considered to be one of the greatest and most influential actors and filmmakers in the history of [Indian cinema](/wiki/Indian_cinema "Indian cinema").[[4]](#cite_note-4) He has been referred to as *The Greatest Showman of [Indian Cinema](/wiki/Cinema_of_India "Cinema of India")*[[5]](#cite_note-5) as well as *The* *[Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin") of Indian Cinema*.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

**Ranbir Raj Kapoor** (pronounced [[raːdʒ kəˈpuːɾ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Hindi_and_Urdu "Help:IPA/Hindi and Urdu")) (14 December 1924 – 2 June 1988[[2]](#cite_note-2)) was an Indian actor, film director, and producer, who worked in [Hindi cinema](/wiki/Hindi_cinema "Hindi cinema").[[3]](#cite_note-3) He is considered to be one of the greatest and most influential actors and filmmakers in the history of [Indian cinema](/wiki/Indian_cinema "Indian cinema").[[4]](#cite_note-4) He has been referred to as *The Greatest Showman of [Indian Cinema](/wiki/Cinema_of_India "Cinema of India")*[[5]](#cite_note-5) as well as *The* *[Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin") of Indian Cinema*.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Born in [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar") as the eldest son of [Prithviraj Kapoor](/wiki/Prithviraj_Kapoor "Prithviraj Kapoor") of the [Kapoor family](/wiki/Kapoor_family "Kapoor family"), Raj Kapoor starred in and produced many films for which he received [multiple accolades](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Raj_Kapoor "List of awards and nominations received by Raj Kapoor"), including three [National Film Awards](/wiki/National_Film_Awards "National Film Awards") and 11 [Filmfare Awards](/wiki/Filmfare_Awards "Filmfare Awards") in India. He was inspired by [Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin") and played characters based on [The Tramp](/wiki/The_Tramp "The Tramp") in films, such as *[Awaara](/wiki/Awaara "Awaara")* (1951), *[Shree 420](/wiki/Shree_420 "Shree 420")* (1955) and *[Mera Naam Joker](/wiki/Mera_Naam_Joker "Mera Naam Joker")* (1970).[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) His performance in *Awaara* was ranked as one of the "Top-Ten Greatest Performances of All Time in World Cinema" by *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* magazine in 2005.[[12]](#cite_note-Time-12) His films *Awaara* (1951) and *[Boot Polish](/wiki/Boot_Polish_(film) "Boot Polish (film)")* (1954) competed for the [Palme d'Or prize](/wiki/Palme_d%27Or "Palme d'Or") at the [Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/Cannes_Film_Festival "Cannes Film Festival") in [1951](/wiki/Cannes_Film_Festival "Cannes Film Festival") and [1955's editions](/wiki/1955_Cannes_Film_Festival "1955 Cannes Film Festival") respectively.[[A]](#cite_note-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15)

His films were global commercial successes in parts of Asia, the Middle East, the Caribbean, Africa, and the [Soviet bloc](/wiki/Soviet_bloc "Soviet bloc").[[15]](#cite_note-16) The [Government of India](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India") honoured him with the [Padma Bhushan](/wiki/Padma_Bhushan "Padma Bhushan") in 1971 for his contributions to [the arts](/wiki/The_arts "The arts").[[16]](#cite_note-Padma_Awards-17) India's highest award in cinema, the [Dadasaheb Phalke Award](/wiki/Dadasaheb_Phalke_Award "Dadasaheb Phalke Award"), was bestowed to him in 1988 by the Government of India.

## Early life and background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raj_Kapoor&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and background")]

Raj Kapoor was born as Ranbir Raj Kapoor on 14 December 1924 at [Kapoor Haveli](/wiki/Kapoor_Haveli "Kapoor Haveli"), a large house then owned by his grandfather in the [Qissa Khwani Bazaar](/wiki/Qissa_Khwani_Bazaar "Qissa Khwani Bazaar") neighborhood of [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"), in the [North-West Frontier Province](/wiki/North-West_Frontier_Province "North-West Frontier Province") of [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India") (now in [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa"), Pakistan), into a [Punjabi Hindu](/wiki/Punjabi_Hindu "Punjabi Hindu") [Khatri](/wiki/Khatri "Khatri") family[[17]](#cite_note-newindianexpress.com-18)[[18]](#cite_note-19). He had an [Arya Samaji](/wiki/Arya_Samaj "Arya Samaj") upbringing.[[19]](#cite_note-20) His parents were [Prithviraj Kapoor](/wiki/Prithviraj_Kapoor "Prithviraj Kapoor") and Ramsarni Devi Kapoor (*née* Mehra). The Kapoor family hailed originally from [Samundri](/wiki/Samundri "Samundri"), a town in the [Lyallpur District](/wiki/Faisalabad_District "Faisalabad District") of the [Punjab Province](/wiki/Punjab_Province_(British_India) "Punjab Province (British India)") of British India (now in the Faisalabad District of [Punjab, Pakistan](/wiki/Punjab,_Pakistan "Punjab, Pakistan")).[[20]](#cite_note-21)

Raj Kapoor was the eldest of six children in the family.[[21]](#cite_note-Ahmed-Pathan-22)[[22]](#cite_note-Kapoor_Family-23) He was the grandson of Dewan Basheshwarnath Kapoor, great-grandson of Dewan Keshavmal Kapoor, and great-great-grandson of Dewan Murli Mal Kapoor, and was part of the [Kapoor family](/wiki/Kapoor_family "Kapoor family"). His brothers were the late actors [Shammi Kapoor](/wiki/Shammi_Kapoor "Shammi Kapoor") (1931–2011) and [Shashi Kapoor](/wiki/Shashi_Kapoor "Shashi Kapoor") (1938–2017). He also had a sister named Urmila Sial (1935–2001). Two other siblings died in infancy. Kapoor's family later on moved from [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar") to [Bombay](/wiki/Bombay "Bombay") for residence and for education.
As Prithviraj moved from city to city early in his career during the 1930s, the family had to move too. Raj Kapoor attended several different schools like [Colonel Brown Cambridge School](/wiki/Colonel_Brown_Cambridge_School "Colonel Brown Cambridge School") in [Dera Doon](/wiki/Dehradun "Dehradun"), [St Xavier's Collegiate School](/wiki/St_Xavier%27s_Collegiate_School "St Xavier's Collegiate School") in [Calcutta](/wiki/Calcutta "Calcutta")[[23]](#cite_note-24) and [Campion School](/wiki/Campion_School,_Mumbai "Campion School, Mumbai") in Bombay.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

### Extended family

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raj_Kapoor&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Extended family")]

He is the nephew of actor [Trilok Kapoor](/wiki/Trilok_Kapoor "Trilok Kapoor"), who is the younger brother of his father. His first cousin was director [Vijay Kapoor](/wiki/Vijay_Kapoor "Vijay Kapoor") who was the son of [Trilok Kapoor](/wiki/Trilok_Kapoor "Trilok Kapoor") and actor [Subbiraj](/wiki/Subbiraj "Subbiraj") who was his father's sister's son. His father's cousin was film producer [Surinder Kapoor](/wiki/Surinder_Kapoor "Surinder Kapoor"), whose children are producer [Boney Kapoor](/wiki/Boney_Kapoor "Boney Kapoor") and actors [Anil Kapoor](/wiki/Anil_Kapoor "Anil Kapoor") and [Sanjay Kapoor](/wiki/Sanjay_Kapoor "Sanjay Kapoor").[[25]](#cite_note-Surinder_Kapoor_&_Prithviraj_Kapoor-26) His father's maternal first cousins were actors [Kamal Kapoor](/wiki/Kamal_Kapoor "Kamal Kapoor"), [Ravindra Kapoor](/wiki/Ravindra_Kapoor "Ravindra Kapoor"), and Nandkishore Kapoor. Kamal's grandson is filmmaker [Goldie Behl](/wiki/Goldie_Behl "Goldie Behl"). His maternal first cousin, Juggal Kishore Mehra, was a singer, whose step-granddaughter, [Salma Agha](/wiki/Salma_Agha "Salma Agha"), later became an actress and singer.

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raj_Kapoor&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Career")]

See also: [Raj Kapoor filmography](/wiki/Raj_Kapoor_filmography "Raj Kapoor filmography")

### Debut and struggle (1947–1948)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raj_Kapoor&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Debut and struggle (1947–1948)")]

At the age of ten, Raj Kapoor appeared in a [Hindi film](/wiki/Hindi_film "Hindi film") for the first time, in 1935 film *Inquilab*.[[26]](#cite_note-27)

Some early success came with the lead role in 1947 with [Kidar Sharma](/wiki/Kidar_Sharma "Kidar Sharma")'s [romantic drama](/wiki/Romance_film "Romance film") *[Neel Kamal](/wiki/Neel_Kamal_(1947_film) "Neel Kamal (1947 film)")* opposite [Begum Para](/wiki/Begum_Para "Begum Para") and [Madhubala](/wiki/Madhubala "Madhubala").[[27]](#cite_note-28) The film proved to be a semi-hit at the box office, but his other releases, such as *[Jail Yatra](/wiki/Jail_Yatra_(1947_film) "Jail Yatra (1947 film)")*, *[Dil Ki Rani](/wiki/Dil_Ki_Rani "Dil Ki Rani")* and *[Chittor Vijay](/wiki/Chittor_Vijay "Chittor Vijay")* didn't do well.[[28]](#cite_note-29) In 1948, he founded his own banner [R. K. Films](/wiki/R._K._Films "R. K. Films") and made his directional debut with the [musical drama](/wiki/Musical_film "Musical film") *[Aag](/wiki/Aag_(1948_film) "Aag (1948 film)")* in which he starred alongside [Nargis](/wiki/Nargis "Nargis"), [Premnath](/wiki/Premnath "Premnath") and [Kamini Kaushal](/wiki/Kamini_Kaushal "Kamini Kaushal").[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-31) The film was an average grosser, but received positive reviews from critics.[[31]](#cite_note-32)

### Stardom (1949–1964)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raj_Kapoor&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Stardom (1949–1964)")]

Several films released in 1949 had a major influence on Kapoor's career.[[32]](#cite_note-33) His first release *[Sunehre Din](/wiki/Sunehre_Din "Sunehre Din")* flopped commercially, but the next one *[Parivartan](/wiki/Parivartan_(film) "Parivartan (film)")* emerged a commercially successful venture while his third release, [Mehboob Khan](/wiki/Mehboob_Khan "Mehboob Khan")'s [romantic drama](/wiki/Romance_film "Romance film") *[Andaz](/wiki/Andaz_(1949_film) "Andaz (1949 film)")*, which also starred [Dilip Kumar](/wiki/Dilip_Kumar "Dilip Kumar") and [Nargis](/wiki/Nargis "Nargis"), went on to become highly successful at the box office.[[33]](#cite_note-34) This was followed by *[Barsaat](/wiki/Barsaat_(1949_film) "Barsaat (1949 film)")*, which he also directed and produced.[[34]](#cite_note-35) The film went ahead of the former, eventually emerging as the highest grossing Indian film of all time, breaking the record of *[Kismet](/wiki/Kismet_(1943_film) "Kismet (1943 film)")*.[[35]](#cite_note-36) It ran in [Kolkata](/wiki/Kolkata "Kolkata")'s [Paradise Cinema](/wiki/Paradise_Cinema_(Kolkata) "Paradise Cinema (Kolkata)") for almost two years.[[36]](#cite_note-37) The huge box office success of *Andaz* and *Barsaat* made Kapoor one of the leading male stars of the time along with Dilip Kumar and [Dev Anand](/wiki/Dev_Anand "Dev Anand").[[37]](#cite_note-Top_Actors-38)

The following year, he starred in *[Sargam](/wiki/Sargam_(1950_film) "Sargam (1950 film)")* and *[Dastan](/wiki/Dastan_(1950_film) "Dastan (1950 film)")* opposite [Rehana](/wiki/Rehana_(actress) "Rehana (actress)") and [Suraiya](/wiki/Suraiya "Suraiya"), respectively.[[38]](#cite_note-39)[[39]](#cite_note-40) Kapoor also acted in the 1950 film [Pyar](/wiki/Pyar "Pyar"), which was not a hit movie and not a popular movie, and did not receive critical acclaim. In that film, the singers were [Kishore Kumar](/wiki/Kishore_Kumar "Kishore Kumar"), [Geeta Dutt](/wiki/Geeta_Dutt "Geeta Dutt"), and [Shamshad Begum](/wiki/Shamshad_Begum "Shamshad Begum"). The film had a total of nine songs, with Kishore Kumar singing five songs which were two solos and three duets. The three duets Kishore had was two duets with Geeta Dutt and one duet with Shmashad Begum. Geeta Dutt singing six songs, which were two duets with Kishore Kumar and four solos, and Shamshad Begum singing only one song which was only one duet with Kishore Kumar. This movie marked the first, and last time Kishore Kumar sang for Raj Kapoor, and it was rare for only one male singer to sing in one film as in most of his films, more than one playback singer sings for him in his films, and Raj Kapoor's major playback singers for him were [Mukesh](/wiki/Mukesh_(singer) "Mukesh (singer)") and [Manna Dey](/wiki/Manna_Dey "Manna Dey").

Kapoor had only one release in 1951 which was his own directional, the [crime drama](/wiki/Crime_film "Crime film") *[Awaara](/wiki/Awaara "Awaara")* co-starring [Prithviraj Kapoor](/wiki/Prithviraj_Kapoor "Prithviraj Kapoor") and Nargis.[[40]](#cite_note-41) The film opened to highly positive response from critics as well as the audience and proved to be another major success for the actor.[[41]](#cite_note-42) Its soundtrack composed by [Shankar–Jaikishan](/wiki/Shankar%E2%80%93Jaikishan "Shankar–Jaikishan") was the best-selling [Hindi film music](/wiki/Hindi_film_music "Hindi film music") album of the 1950s and became popular in foreign markets as well, especially in the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), [China](/wiki/China "China"), [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") and [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan").[[42]](#cite_note-43)[[43]](#cite_note-44) *Awaara* also earned Kapoor a fan-following in Soviet Union where the film had approximately 100 million admissions and remains the third-most watched foreign film in the country.[[44]](#cite_note-45) In 1952, he reunited with Nargis for [psychological drama](/wiki/Psychological_fiction "Psychological fiction") *[Anhonee](/wiki/Anhonee_(1952_film) "Anhonee (1952 film)")* and [crime noir](/wiki/Crime_film "Crime film") *[Bewafa](/wiki/Bewafa_(1952_film) "Bewafa (1952 film)")*.[[45]](#cite_note-hindu1-46)[[46]](#cite_note-47) While *Anhonee* was a success, *Bewafa* only managed average returns.[[47]](#cite_note-48) This was followed by a minor setback, the next year as none of his films, including *[Aah](/wiki/Aah_(film) "Aah (film)")* and *[Dhoon](/wiki/Dhoon "Dhoon")* worked at the box office.[[48]](#cite_note-49)

After having no major appearance in 1954, he made a comeback in 1955 with the [comedy drama](/wiki/Comedy_(genre) "Comedy (genre)") film *[Shree 420](/wiki/Shree_420 "Shree 420")*, co-starring Nargis and [Nadira](/wiki/Nadira_(Indian_actress) "Nadira (Indian actress)"),[[49]](#cite_note-50) which garnered critical acclaim and emerged the [top-grossing film of the year](/wiki/List_of_Hindi_films_of_1955 "List of Hindi films of 1955").[[50]](#cite_note-51) Its songs, such as "[Mera Joota Hai Japani](/wiki/Mera_Joota_Hai_Japani "Mera Joota Hai Japani")", a solo by [Mukesh](/wiki/Mukesh_(singer) "Mukesh (singer)"), "Pyar Hua Iqrar Hua", a duet by [Manna Dey](/wiki/Manna_Dey "Manna Dey") and [Lata Mangeshkar](/wiki/Lata_Mangeshkar "Lata Mangeshkar"), and ""Mud Mud Ke Na Dekh", a duet by Dey and [Asha Bhosle](/wiki/Asha_Bhosle "Asha Bhosle"), topped the year-end annual list of [Binaca Geetmala](/wiki/Binaca_Geetmala "Binaca Geetmala") and made its soundtrack the fourth best-selling [Bollywood](/wiki/Hindi_film_music "Hindi film music") album of the 1950s.[[51]](#cite_note-Screen-52)[[52]](#cite_note-Music-53) *Shree 420* was released in the Soviet Union in 1956 and it took 2nd spot at the [Soviet box office charts](/wiki/List_of_Soviet_films_of_the_year_by_ticket_sales "List of Soviet films of the year by ticket sales") that year.[[53]](#cite_note-54) Despite being imported at an unusually high price, it was the most successful foreign film of the year at the Soviet box office, drawing an audience of 35 million viewers.[[54]](#cite_note-indiana163-55) At the [3rd National Awards](/wiki/3rd_National_Film_Awards "3rd National Film Awards"), it won [National Award for Second Best Feature Film in Hindi](/wiki/National_Film_Award_for_Best_Hindi_Feature_Film "National Film Award for Best Hindi Feature Film").[[55]](#cite_note-56) The following year, he starred in Anant Thakur's *[Chori Chori](/wiki/Chori_Chori_(1956_film) "Chori Chori (1956 film)")* and [Sombhu Mitra](/wiki/Sombhu_Mitra "Sombhu Mitra")'s *[Jagte Raho](/wiki/Jagte_Raho "Jagte Raho")* (which he also produced).[[56]](#cite_note-57) Although underperforming commercially, the latter received acclaim and its [Bengali](/wiki/Ek_Din_Raatre "Ek Din Raatre") version won Certificate of Merit at the [4th National Film Awards](/wiki/4th_National_Film_Awards "4th National Film Awards").[[57]](#cite_note-4thaward-58) *Chori Chori* and *Jagte Raho* also proved to be the final film appearances of Nargis opposite Kapoor.[[58]](#cite_note-59)

After delivering a moderate fare with *[Sharada](/wiki/Sharada_(1957_film) "Sharada (1957 film)")* in 1957, Kapoor delivered two successful films, the next year - *[Phir Subha Hogi](/wiki/Phir_Subha_Hogi "Phir Subha Hogi")* and *[Parvarish](/wiki/Parvarish_(1958_film) "Parvarish (1958 film)")*, both co-starring [Mala Sinha](/wiki/Mala_Sinha "Mala Sinha").[[59]](#cite_note-60)[[60]](#cite_note-61) In 1959, he collaborated with [Hrishikesh Mukherjee](/wiki/Hrishikesh_Mukherjee "Hrishikesh Mukherjee") for the [comedy drama](/wiki/Comedy "Comedy") film *[Anari](/wiki/Anari_(1959_film) "Anari (1959 film)")*, which also had [Nutan](/wiki/Nutan "Nutan"), [Motilal](/wiki/Motilal_(actor) "Motilal (actor)") and [Lalita Pawar](/wiki/Lalita_Pawar "Lalita Pawar") in the lead.[[61]](#cite_note-62) The film took number one spot at the box office that year and was a blockbuster for Mukherjee whose last directional *[Musafir](/wiki/Musafir_(1957_film) "Musafir (1957 film)")* received lukewarm response from audience.[[62]](#cite_note-63)[[63]](#cite_note-64) The soundtrack of *Anari* became one of the best-selling Hindi film album of the decade. Especially the song "Kisi Ki Muskurahaton Pe", a solo by Mukesh, was very popular.[[52]](#cite_note-Music-53) For his performance in the film, Kapoor won his first [Filmfare Award for Best Actor](/wiki/Filmfare_Award_for_Best_Actor "Filmfare Award for Best Actor").[[64]](#cite_note-65)

Kapoor began the 1960s with [Radhu Karmakar](/wiki/Radhu_Karmakar "Radhu Karmakar")'s *[Jis Desh Mein Ganga Behti Hai](/wiki/Jis_Desh_Mein_Ganga_Behti_Hai "Jis Desh Mein Ganga Behti Hai")* (which he also produced) and [Manmohan Desai](/wiki/Manmohan_Desai "Manmohan Desai")'s *[Chhalia](/wiki/Chhalia "Chhalia")*. The former did very well at the box office.[[65]](#cite_note-Boxoffice-66) It won Kapoor several accolades, including [National Award for Second Best Feature Film in Hindi](/wiki/National_Film_Award_for_Best_Hindi_Feature_Film "National Film Award for Best Hindi Feature Film"), [Filmfare Award for Best Film](/wiki/Filmfare_Award_for_Best_Film "Filmfare Award for Best Film") and his second [Filmfare Award for Best Actor](/wiki/Filmfare_Award_for_Best_Actor "Filmfare Award for Best Actor").[[66]](#cite_note-67)[[67]](#cite_note-68) On the other hand, *Chhalia* based on [Fyodor Dostoevsky](/wiki/Fyodor_Dostoevsky "Fyodor Dostoevsky")'s "[White Nights](/wiki/White_Nights_(short_story) "White Nights (short story)")" was a moderate commercial success, but received positive response from critics.[[68]](#cite_note-69) From 1961 to 1963, he had only one success in [C. V. Sridhar](/wiki/C._V._Sridhar "C. V. Sridhar")'s [melodrama](/wiki/Melodrama "Melodrama") film *[Nazrana](/wiki/Nazrana_(1961_film) "Nazrana (1961 film)")* (1961).[[69]](#cite_note-70) In 1964, Kapoor had two releases, *[Dulha Dulhan](/wiki/Dulha_Dulhan "Dulha Dulhan")* opposite [Sadhana](/wiki/Sadhana_Shivdasani "Sadhana Shivdasani") and his own directional *[Sangam](/wiki/Sangam_(1964_Hindi_film) "Sangam (1964 Hindi film)")*, co-starring [Vyjayanthimala](/wiki/Vyjayanthimala "Vyjayanthimala") and [Rajendra Kumar](/wiki/Rajendra_Kumar "Rajendra Kumar"), which was the first [Indian film](/wiki/Cinema_of_India "Cinema of India") to be exclusively shot abroad on locations including [London](/wiki/London "London"), [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") and [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") and was also among the most expensive film of its time with the [longest runtime for an Indian film](/wiki/List_of_longest_films_in_India#List "List of longest films in India") up to that time.[[70]](#cite_note-Sangam-71) *Sangam*, which was Kapoor's first [color film](/wiki/Color_motion_picture_film "Color motion picture film"), was the second-highest-grossing film of the 1960s behind *[Mughal-E-Azam](/wiki/Mughal-E-Azam "Mughal-E-Azam")*.[[71]](#cite_note-72) Its soundtrack, composed by Shankar-Jaikishan, became very popular and included the songs "Dost Dost Na Raha", "Har Dil Jo Pyaar Karega", "Bol Radha Bol", "Yeh Mera Prem Patra".[[72]](#cite_note-73)
*Sangam* also proved to be one of the last major hits of Kapoor as a lead.[[70]](#cite_note-Sangam-71)

### Decline, focus on direction and further acting assignments (1965–1988)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raj_Kapoor&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Decline, focus on direction and further acting assignments (1965–1988)")]

Kapoor lost stardom in the late-1960s as his subsequent releases, such as *[Teesri Kasam](/wiki/Teesri_Kasam "Teesri Kasam")* (1966), *[Diwana](/wiki/Diwana_(1967_film) "Diwana (1967 film)")* (1967), *[Around the World](/wiki/Around_the_World_(1967_film) "Around the World (1967 film)")* (1967) and *[Sapnon Ka Saudagar](/wiki/Sapnon_Ka_Saudagar "Sapnon Ka Saudagar")* (1968) were commercially unsuccessful.[[73]](#cite_note-74)
In 1970, he starred, produced and directed *[Mera Naam Joker](/wiki/Mera_Naam_Joker "Mera Naam Joker")*, which took six years in making.[[74]](#cite_note-Cult-75) The film had an ensemble cast consisting of [Simi Garewal](/wiki/Simi_Garewal "Simi Garewal"), [Kseniya Ryabinkina](/wiki/Kseniya_Ryabinkina "Kseniya Ryabinkina"), [Padmini](/wiki/Padmini_(actress) "Padmini (actress)"), [Manoj Kumar](/wiki/Manoj_Kumar "Manoj Kumar"), [Dharmendra](/wiki/Dharmendra "Dharmendra"), Rajendra Kumar and [Rishi Kapoor](/wiki/Rishi_Kapoor "Rishi Kapoor") (playing younger version of Kapoor). Upon release, *Mera Naam Joker* was heavily panned by critics and proved to be a [box office bomb](/wiki/Box_Office_bomb "Box Office bomb"), eventually putting Kapoor into a financial crisis.[[74]](#cite_note-Cult-75) However, in later years, it attained cult status and is now considered one of his best films with film experts labelling it a "misunderstood masterpiece".[[75]](#cite_note-76) For portraying a kind-hearted clown in the film, Kapoor won [BFJA Award for Best Actor (Hindi)](/wiki/BFJA_Award_for_Best_Actor_(Hindi) "BFJA Award for Best Actor (Hindi)").[[76]](#cite_note-77)

The following year, Kapoor produced and appeared alongside his son ([Randhir Kapoor](/wiki/Randhir_Kapoor "Randhir Kapoor")) and father in *[Kal Aaj Aur Kal](/wiki/Kal_Aaj_Aur_Kal "Kal Aaj Aur Kal")*.[[77]](#cite_note-78) The film proved to be moderately successful and marked Shankar-Jaikishan's last collaboration with R. K. Films.[[78]](#cite_note-79) In 1973, he decided to launch Rishi to cover-up the losses incurred due to the box office failure of *Mera Naam Joker*,[[79]](#cite_note-dnaindia.com-80) in the [romantic musical](/wiki/Romance_film "Romance film") *[Bobby](/wiki/Bobby_(1973_film) "Bobby (1973 film)")*, which paired him opposite debutante [Dimple Kapadia](/wiki/Dimple_Kapadia "Dimple Kapadia").[[80]](#cite_note-81) The film opened to thunderous commercial response, both in India as well as overseas, and emerged as the highest-grossing film of 1973, as well as one of the highest grossing to that point.[[81]](#cite_note-Rishi-82)[[82]](#cite_note-hindustantimes-83) In 1975, Kapoor returned to acting with two back-to-back successful films in Randhir Kapoor's *[Dharam Karam](/wiki/Dharam_Karam "Dharam Karam")* and Naresh Kumar's *[Do Jasoos](/wiki/Do_Jasoos "Do Jasoos")*.[[83]](#cite_note-Alimchand-84) In 1978, he returned to the director's chair with *[Satyam Shivam Sundaram](/wiki/Satyam_Shivam_Sundaram "Satyam Shivam Sundaram")* starring [Shashi Kapoor](/wiki/Shashi_Kapoor "Shashi Kapoor") and [Zeenat Aman](/wiki/Zeenat_Aman "Zeenat Aman").[[84]](#cite_note-85) It ran into controversies and was accused of promoting "obscenity", but opened to a positive reception from the critics as well as the audience and earned him a nomination in the [Filmfare Award for Best Director](/wiki/Filmfare_Award_for_Best_Director "Filmfare Award for Best Director") category.[[85]](#cite_note-86)[[86]](#cite_note-87)

In 1980, Kapoor appeared alongside [Sanjay Khan](/wiki/Sanjay_Khan "Sanjay Khan") and Zeenat Aman in the highly anticipated romantic drama *[Abdullah](/wiki/Abdullah_(film) "Abdullah (film)")*.[[87]](#cite_note-88) Upon release, it did not live up to expectations and underperformed domestically, but was successful overseas due to his popularity in the Soviet Union.[[88]](#cite_note-89) In 1982, Kapoor once again directed his son Rishi in the romantic musical drama *[Prem Rog](/wiki/Prem_Rog "Prem Rog")* opposite [Padmini Kolhapure](/wiki/Padmini_Kolhapure "Padmini Kolhapure").[[89]](#cite_note-90) The film marked his return to social themes and emerged commercially successful at the box office as the [second-highest-grossing film of the year](/wiki/List_of_Hindi_films_of_1982 "List of Hindi films of 1982").[[90]](#cite_note-91)[[91]](#cite_note-92)[[81]](#cite_note-Rishi-82) Its soundtrack composed by [Laxmikant-Pyarelal](/wiki/Laxmikant-Pyarelal "Laxmikant-Pyarelal") was also very successful and proved to be one of the best-selling Hindi film albums of the decade.[[92]](#cite_note-boi80s-93) The same year, he appeared alongside Shashi in [Asit Sen](/wiki/Asit_Sen_(director) "Asit Sen (director)")'s *[Vakil Babu](/wiki/Vakil_Babu "Vakil Babu")*, which marked his final film appearance.[[93]](#cite_note-94)

In 1985, he directed and produced the romance film *[Ram Teri Ganga Maili](/wiki/Ram_Teri_Ganga_Maili "Ram Teri Ganga Maili")*, which had his youngest son [Rajiv Kapoor](/wiki/Rajiv_Kapoor "Rajiv Kapoor") and the then newcomer [Mandakini](/wiki/Mandakini_(actress) "Mandakini (actress)") playing the lead roles.[[94]](#cite_note-95) Despite not having any stars, the film took first spot at the box office that year and proved to be one of the biggest hits of the 1980s.[[95]](#cite_note-96)[[96]](#cite_note-97) At the [33rd Filmfare Awards](/wiki/33rd_Filmfare_Awards "33rd Filmfare Awards"), it won five awards, including the [Best Film](/wiki/Filmfare_Award_for_Best_Film "Filmfare Award for Best Film"), [Best Director](/wiki/Filmfare_Award_for_Best_Director "Filmfare Award for Best Director") for Kapoor and [Best Music Director](/wiki/Filmfare_Award_for_Best_Music_Director "Filmfare Award for Best Music Director") for [Ravindra Jain](/wiki/Ravindra_Jain "Ravindra Jain").[[97]](#cite_note-98) The film was Kapoor's last before his death in 1988. A few months before his death, he had made a film with legendary actor [Ashok Kumar](/wiki/Ashok_Kumar "Ashok Kumar"), "Chor Mandali" (1986), but it was never released due to legal issues. Although the movie is unreleased, its songs are available. He was also working on an [Indo-Pak](/wiki/Indo-Pak "Indo-Pak") cross-border love story *[Henna](/wiki/Henna_(film) "Henna (film)")*, which was later directed and released by his son Randhir in 1991 and went on to become a major box office success.[[98]](#cite_note-99)[[81]](#cite_note-Rishi-82)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raj_Kapoor&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Personal life")]

On 12 May 1946, Raj Kapoor married Krishna Malhotra (1930–2018), a [first cousin](/wiki/Cousin_marriage "Cousin marriage") of his father Prithviraj.[[99]](#cite_note-100)[[100]](#cite_note-101) She was then 16 and he was 22.[[101]](#cite_note-102) Krishna was one of nine siblings, and following her marriage, many of them joined the Hindi film industry. Her brothers, [Rajendra Nath](/wiki/Rajendra_Nath "Rajendra Nath"), [Prem Nath](/wiki/Prem_Nath "Prem Nath") and [Narendra Nath](/wiki/Narendra_Nath "Narendra Nath"), later became actors, and her sister Uma is married to actor [Prem Chopra](/wiki/Prem_Chopra "Prem Chopra").[[102]](#cite_note-103)

Raj and Krishna Kapoor had five children: three sons, actors [Randhir Kapoor](/wiki/Randhir_Kapoor "Randhir Kapoor"), [Rishi Kapoor](/wiki/Rishi_Kapoor "Rishi Kapoor") and [Rajiv Kapoor](/wiki/Rajiv_Kapoor "Rajiv Kapoor"), and two daughters, Ritu Nanda and Rima Jain. Randhir is married to former actress [Babita](/wiki/Babita "Babita") and is the father of actresses [Karisma Kapoor](/wiki/Karisma_Kapoor "Karisma Kapoor") and [Kareena Kapoor](/wiki/Kareena_Kapoor "Kareena Kapoor"). Rishi's widow is actress [Neetu Singh](/wiki/Neetu_Singh "Neetu Singh") and their two children are daughter Riddhima Kapoor, and son, actor [Ranbir Kapoor](/wiki/Ranbir_Kapoor "Ranbir Kapoor"). Raj Kapoor's elder daughter, Ritu Nanda, was married to industrialist Rajan Nanda (scion of the family which promoted and controls the [Escorts group](/wiki/Escorts_group "Escorts group")), and they had two children: daughter Nitasha Nanda and son, [Nikhil Nanda](/wiki/Nikhil_Nanda "Nikhil Nanda"), married to [Shweta](/wiki/Shweta_Bachchan_Nanda "Shweta Bachchan Nanda"), daughter of actors [Amitabh Bachchan](/wiki/Amitabh_Bachchan "Amitabh Bachchan") and [Jaya Bachchan](/wiki/Jaya_Bachchan "Jaya Bachchan"). Raj Kapoor's younger daughter, Rima Jain, is married to investment banker Manoj Jain and is the mother of Armaan Jain and Aadar Jain, both actors.

Both of Kapoor's brothers, all three of Kapoor's sons, two of Kapoor's daughters-in-law, three of Kapoor's grandchildren and a great-grandson are all actors in the Hindi film industry. His granddaughters Karisma Kapoor and Kareena Kapoor (daughters of Kapoor's eldest son Randhir), and grandson Ranbir Kapoor (son of Kapoor's second son Rishi) are the latest [Hindi cinema](/wiki/Hindi_cinema "Hindi cinema") superstars from the Kapoor family, while another of his grandsons, Nikhil Nanda (Kapoor's daughter Ritu's son), is a noted industrialist. Nikhil's son, [Agastya Nanda](/wiki/Agastya_Nanda "Agastya Nanda"), made his acting debut with *[The Archies](/wiki/The_Archies_(film) "The Archies (film)")* in 2023.[[103]](#cite_note-104)

Kapoor had a longtime romantic relationship with renowned actress [Nargis Dutt](/wiki/Nargis_Dutt "Nargis Dutt") during the 1940s and 1950s, despite being a married man, although neither ever publicly admitted to this.[[104]](#cite_note-105) The couple starred in several films together, including *[Awaara](/wiki/Awaara "Awaara")* and *[Shree 420](/wiki/Shree_420 "Shree 420")*. As Kapoor would not leave his wife and children, Nargis ended their relationship after *[Chori Chori](/wiki/Chori_Chori_(1956_film) "Chori Chori (1956 film)")* and married her co-star [Sunil Dutt](/wiki/Sunil_Dutt "Sunil Dutt") with whom she fell in love on the set of *[Mother India](/wiki/Mother_India "Mother India")* (1957). In 2017, his second son Rishi confirmed his father's affairs in his autobiography *Khullam Khulla*.[[105]](#cite_note-106)[[106]](#cite_note-107)[[107]](#cite_note-108)

Krishna Raj Kapoor died on 1 October 2018. Rajan Nanda, husband of Ritu Nanda, died on 5 August 2018. In January 2020, Ritu Nanda herself died. She was followed three months later by her younger brother [Rishi](/wiki/Rishi_Kapoor "Rishi Kapoor"), Kapoor's second son, who died in April 2020. Kapoor's third and youngest son, [Rajiv](/wiki/Rajiv_Kapoor "Rajiv Kapoor"), died in February 2021.[[108]](#cite_note-109)[[109]](#cite_note-110)[[110]](#cite_note-111)

[Dilip Kumar](/wiki/Dilip_Kumar "Dilip Kumar"), [Mukesh](/wiki/Mukesh_(singer) "Mukesh (singer)"), [Shailendra](/wiki/Shailendra_(lyricist) "Shailendra (lyricist)"), [Hasrat Jaipuri](/wiki/Hasrat_Jaipuri "Hasrat Jaipuri"), [Dev Anand](/wiki/Dev_Anand "Dev Anand"), [Pran](/wiki/Pran_(actor) "Pran (actor)"), [Rajendra Kumar](/wiki/Rajendra_Kumar "Rajendra Kumar"), [Manna Dey](/wiki/Manna_Dey "Manna Dey"), [Shankar Jaikishan](/wiki/Shankar_Jaikishan "Shankar Jaikishan"), [Hrishikesh Mukherjee](/wiki/Hrishikesh_Mukherjee "Hrishikesh Mukherjee"), [Khwaja Ahmad Abbas](/wiki/Khwaja_Ahmad_Abbas "Khwaja Ahmad Abbas") and [Rajesh Khanna](/wiki/Rajesh_Khanna "Rajesh Khanna") were among Kapoor's closest friends from the movie industry.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raj_Kapoor&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Death")]

### Cause

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raj_Kapoor&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Cause")]

Raj Kapoor suffered from [asthma](/wiki/Asthma "Asthma") in his later years; he died of complications related to the disease in 1988 at the age of 63. He collapsed at the event where he was to receive the [Dadasaheb Phalke Award](/wiki/Dadasaheb_Phalke_Award "Dadasaheb Phalke Award") in [New Delhi](/wiki/New_Delhi "New Delhi"), and was taken to the [All India Institute of Medical Sciences, New Delhi](/wiki/All_India_Institute_of_Medical_Sciences,_New_Delhi "All India Institute of Medical Sciences, New Delhi") for treatment. He was hospitalised for about a month before he succumbed to complications arising from his asthma.[[111]](#cite_note-greatest_showman-112) At the time of his death, he was working on the movie *[Henna](/wiki/Henna_(film) "Henna (film)")* (an Indo-Pakistan based love story). The film was later completed by his sons [Randhir Kapoor](/wiki/Randhir_Kapoor "Randhir Kapoor") and [Rishi Kapoor](/wiki/Rishi_Kapoor "Rishi Kapoor") and was released in 1991.

### Memorial at family farm in Pune

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raj_Kapoor&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Memorial at family farm in Pune")]

His and his parents' [samadhi](/wiki/Samadhi_(shrine) "Samadhi (shrine)") is at their family farm "Rajbaugh", meaning the "gardens of king". Located inside the MIT Art Design and Technology University (MIT ADTU), Rajbaugh lies on the banks of [Mula-Mutha River](/wiki/Mula-Mutha_River "Mula-Mutha River") in [Loni Kalbhor](/wiki/Loni_Kalbhor "Loni Kalbhor") village 30 km east of [Pune](/wiki/Pune "Pune") in Maharashtra.Kapoor family sold part of 125 acres Rajbaugh to MIT ADTU which built a memorial for the Kapoor family on its campus. The memorial was unveiled in 2014 in the presence of [Lata Mangeshkar](/wiki/Lata_Mangeshkar "Lata Mangeshkar") and Kapoor clan. The Kapoor family memorial has 7 pagodas showing elements of Raj Kapoor's movies, a museum or viewing gallery which showcases family photographs and moments from his movie making from 1945 to 1990. Raj Kapoor shot many of his films at this farm, including *[Satyam Shivam Sundaram](/wiki/Satyam_Shivam_Sundaram "Satyam Shivam Sundaram")*, *[Mera Naam Joker](/wiki/Mera_Naam_Joker "Mera Naam Joker")*, *[Bobby](/wiki/Bobby_(1973_film) "Bobby (1973 film)")*, and *[Prem Rog](/wiki/Prem_Rog "Prem Rog")*. The family bungalow inside the farm has been preserved; the popular song "[Hum Tum Ek Kamre Mein Band Ho](/wiki/Hum_Tum_Ek_Kamre_Mein_Band_Ho "Hum Tum Ek Kamre Mein Band Ho")" was shot inside this bungalow.[[112]](#cite_note-cameo11-113)[[113]](#cite_note-cameo12-114)[[114]](#cite_note-cameo13-115)[[115]](#cite_note-cameo10-116)[[116]](#cite_note-cameo14-117)

## Artistry and legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raj_Kapoor&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Artistry and legacy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/%D0%A0%D0%B0%D0%B4%D0%B6_%D0%9A%D0%B0%D0%BF%D1%83%D1%80-_%D0%B2%D1%81%D1%82%D1%80%D0%B5%D1%87%D0%B0_%D0%B2_%D0%98%D0%BD%D0%B4%D0%B8%D0%B8_%D1%81_%D0%B4%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B5%D0%B3%D0%B0%D1%86%D0%B8%D0%B5%D0%B9_%D1%83%D0%B7%D0%B1%D0%B5%D0%BA%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B8%D1%85_%D0%BF%D0%B8%D1%81%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B5%D0%B9-_%D0%A1%D0%B0%D0%BC%D0%B8%D0%B3_%D0%90%D0%B1%D0%B4%D1%83%D0%BA%D0%B0%D1%85%D1%85%D0%B0%D1%80_%D0%B8_%D0%B4%D1%80%2C_1950-%D1%8B%D0%B5.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%A0%D0%B0%D0%B4%D0%B6_%D0%9A%D0%B0%D0%BF%D1%83%D1%80-_%D0%B2%D1%81%D1%82%D1%80%D0%B5%D1%87%D0%B0_%D0%B2_%D0%98%D0%BD%D0%B4%D0%B8%D0%B8_%D1%81_%D0%B4%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B5%D0%B3%D0%B0%D1%86%D0%B8%D0%B5%D0%B9_%D1%83%D0%B7%D0%B1%D0%B5%D0%BA%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B8%D1%85_%D0%BF%D0%B8%D1%81%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B5%D0%B9-_%D0%A1%D0%B0%D0%BC%D0%B8%D0%B3_%D0%90%D0%B1%D0%B4%D1%83%D0%BA%D0%B0%D1%85%D1%85%D0%B0%D1%80_%D0%B8_%D0%B4%D1%80,_1950-%D1%8B%D0%B5.jpg)

Raj Kapoor alongside a delegation of writers from Soviet Uzbekistan ([Sami Abduqahhor](/wiki/Sami_Abduqahhor "Sami Abduqahhor") etc.), 1950s, India

Raj Kapoor is often referred as *The Greatest Showman of Indian Cinema* in the Indian media.[[117]](#cite_note-118)[[118]](#cite_note-119)[[119]](#cite_note-120) [Film historians](/wiki/History_of_cinema "History of cinema") and movie buffs speak of him as the "[Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin") of [Indian cinema](/wiki/Indian_cinema "Indian cinema")", since he often portrayed a tramp-like figure, who, despite adversity, was still cheerful and honest. His movies were popular in large parts of South/Central/Southeast Asia, the former [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union")/[Bloc](/wiki/Soviet_bloc "Soviet bloc"), China, the Middle East, and Africa; his movies were global commercial successes.

[Siddharth Kak](/wiki/Siddharth_Kak "Siddharth Kak") made *Raj Kapoor*, a feature film documentary on the actor in 1987 which was produced by the [Government of India](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India")'s [Films Division](/wiki/Films_Division "Films Division").[[120]](#cite_note-121)

A postage stamp, bearing his face, was released by [India Post](/wiki/India_Post "India Post") to honour him on 14 December 2001. To honour him, a brass statue of his was unveiled at [Walk of the Stars](/wiki/Walk_of_the_Stars "Walk of the Stars") at [Bandra Bandstand](/wiki/Bandra_Bandstand "Bandra Bandstand") in [Mumbai](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai") in March 2012.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Raj_Kapoor_2001_stamp_of_India.jpg/250px-Raj_Kapoor_2001_stamp_of_India.jpg)](/wiki/File:Raj_Kapoor_2001_stamp_of_India.jpg)

Raj Kapoor 2001 stamp of India

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Raj_Kapoor%27s_statue_at_Bombay%27s_Walk_of_Stars.jpg/250px-Raj_Kapoor%27s_statue_at_Bombay%27s_Walk_of_Stars.jpg)](/wiki/File:Raj_Kapoor%27s_statue_at_Bombay%27s_Walk_of_Stars.jpg)

Kapoor's statue at [Walk of the Stars](/wiki/Walk_of_the_Stars "Walk of the Stars")

Many of Raj Kapoor's movies had a patriotic theme. His films *[Aag](/wiki/Aag_(1948_film) "Aag (1948 film)")*, *[Shree 420](/wiki/Shree_420 "Shree 420")* and *[Jis Desh Men Ganga Behti Hai](/wiki/Jis_Desh_Men_Ganga_Behti_Hai "Jis Desh Men Ganga Behti Hai")* (In the country where the Ganges flows) celebrated the newly independent India, and encouraged film-goers to be patriots. Raj Kapoor commissioned these famous lyrics for *[Mera Joota Hai Japani](/wiki/Mera_Joota_Hai_Japani "Mera Joota Hai Japani")*, a song from the movie *[Shree 420](/wiki/Shree_420 "Shree 420")*:

:   *Mera joota hai Japani* (My shoes are Japanese)
:   *Ye patloon Inglistani* (These trousers are English)
:   *Sar pe lal topi Roosi* (The red cap on my head is Russian)
:   *Phir bhi dil hai Hindustani* (But still, however, my heart is Indian)

The song is still extremely popular and has been featured in a number of movies since *[Shree 420](/wiki/Shree_420 "Shree 420")* was released. Indian author [Mahasweta Devi](/wiki/Mahasweta_Devi "Mahasweta Devi") stopped the show with her inaugural speech at the 2006 [Frankfurt Book Fair](/wiki/Frankfurt_Book_Fair "Frankfurt Book Fair") when she used these lyrics to express her own heartfelt patriotism and debt to her country.

Kapoor appeared in *[Box Office India](/wiki/Box_Office_India "Box Office India")*'s "Top Actors" list twelve times from 1949 to 1960.[[37]](#cite_note-Top_Actors-38)

Apart from his success as an actor, Kapoor is also considered one of the greatest directors of [Indian cinema](/wiki/Indian_cinema "Indian cinema").[[121]](#cite_note-122) The films directed by him, such as *[Shree 420](/wiki/Shree_420 "Shree 420")* (1955), *[Sangam](/wiki/Sangam_(1964_Hindi_film) "Sangam (1964 Hindi film)")* (1964), *[Bobby](/wiki/Bobby_(1973_film) "Bobby (1973 film)")* (1973) and *[Ram Teri Ganga Maili](/wiki/Ram_Teri_Ganga_Maili "Ram Teri Ganga Maili")* (1985) are among the biggest hits of all time with each one having footfalls ranging from 40 to 50 million or even more.[[81]](#cite_note-Rishi-82)[[122]](#cite_note-123)

In 2014, [Google](/wiki/Google_Doodle "Google Doodle") commemorated his 90th birthday.[[123]](#cite_note-124)

Raj Kapoor was a canny judge of [filmi](/wiki/Filmi "Filmi") music and lyrics. Many of the songs he commissioned are evergreen hits. He introduced the music directors [Shankar–Jaikishan](/wiki/Shankar%E2%80%93Jaikishan "Shankar–Jaikishan") and the lyricists [Hasrat Jaipuri](/wiki/Hasrat_Jaipuri "Hasrat Jaipuri") and [Shailendra](/wiki/Shailendra_(lyricist) "Shailendra (lyricist)"). He is also remembered for his strong sense of visual style. He used striking visual compositions, elaborate sets, and dramatic lighting to complete the mood set by the music. He introduced the actresses [Nimmi](/wiki/Nimmi "Nimmi"), [Dimple Kapadia](/wiki/Dimple_Kapadia "Dimple Kapadia"), and [Mandakini](/wiki/Mandakini_(actress) "Mandakini (actress)"), as well as launching and reviving the careers of his sons Rishi, Randhir and Rajiv.

Famous for making his actresses reveal the body, not very common then in Indian cinema, it was said his 'show-womanship' matched his showmanship.[[124]](#cite_note-BharatanMelody-125) He often portrayed women through a deeply sensual and symbolic lens, frequently invoking themes of what he calls "sacred nudity" (*muqaddas uriyan* in [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu")), a concept he linked to early memories and which manifested in recurring bathing scenes and ethereal female imagery in his films, as he recounts:

> I was extremely precocious. And I was a worshipper of nudity. I think it all started because of my intimacy with my mother who was young, beautiful, and had the sharp features of a [Pathan](/wiki/Pashtuns "Pashtuns") woman. We often bathed together, and seeing her in the nude must have left a deep erotic impression on my mind. There is an excellent Urdu phrase, *muqaddas uriyan* (sacred nudity), which describes this perfectly. In my films, bathing scenes recur often. Women in general occupy most of my early memories, and they appear in my films like ghosts that refuse to be buried.[[125]](#cite_note-126)

Raj Kapoor's *[Shree 420](/wiki/Shree_420 "Shree 420")* (1955) movie's [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") song *[Mera Joota Hai Japani](/wiki/Mera_Joota_Hai_Japani "Mera Joota Hai Japani")* ("My Shoes are Japanese") was used in opening sequence of [20th Century Studios](/wiki/20th_Century_Studios "20th Century Studios") 2016 movie *[Deadpool](/wiki/Deadpool_(film) "Deadpool (film)")*, starring [Ryan Reynolds](/wiki/Ryan_Reynolds "Ryan Reynolds").[[126]](#cite_note-127)

The 1967 "Song about Yogis" ([Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"): Песенка про йогов) by [Vladimir Vysotsky](/wiki/Vladimir_Vysotsky "Vladimir Vysotsky") mentions Raj Kapoor as one of the three best-known symbols of Indian culture in the Soviet Union, along with [Shiva](/wiki/Shiva "Shiva") and [yoga](/wiki/Yoga "Yoga").[[127]](#cite_note-128)

In 2022, he was placed in *[Outlook India](/wiki/Outlook_India "Outlook India")*'s "75 Best Bollywood Actors" list.[[128]](#cite_note-129)

## Awards and honors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raj_Kapoor&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Awards and honors")]

Main article: [List of awards and nominations received by Raj Kapoor](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Raj_Kapoor "List of awards and nominations received by Raj Kapoor")

Kapoor had received many awards throughout his career, including 3 [National Film Awards](/wiki/National_Film_Awards "National Film Awards"), 11 [Filmfare Awards](/wiki/Filmfare_Awards "Filmfare Awards") and 21 nominations. His films *[Awaara](/wiki/Awaara "Awaara")* (1951) and *[Boot Polish](/wiki/Boot_Polish_(film) "Boot Polish (film)")* (1954) were nominated for the [Palme d'Or](/wiki/Palme_d%27Or "Palme d'Or") at the [Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/Cannes_Film_Festival "Cannes Film Festival"). His acting in the former was rated as one of the "Top Ten Performances of All Time in World Cinema" by [Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)") magazine.[[12]](#cite_note-Time-12) His film *[Jagte Raho](/wiki/Jagte_Raho "Jagte Raho")* (1956) also won the [Crystal Globe](/wiki/Crystal_Globe_(Karlovy_Vary_International_Film_Festival) "Crystal Globe (Karlovy Vary International Film Festival)") award at the [Karlovy Vary International Film Festival](/wiki/Karlovy_Vary_International_Film_Festival "Karlovy Vary International Film Festival").

The [Government of India](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India") honoured him with the [Padma Bhushan](/wiki/Padma_Bhushan "Padma Bhushan") in 1971 and the [Dadasaheb Phalke Award](/wiki/Dadasaheb_Phalke_Award "Dadasaheb Phalke Award") in 1987 – the highest award for cinematic excellence in India. In 2001, he was honoured with "Best Director of the Millennium" by [Stardust Awards](/wiki/Stardust_Awards "Stardust Awards"). He was named "Showman of the Millennium" by [Star Screen Awards](/wiki/Star_Screen_Awards "Star Screen Awards") in 2002.

In June 2011, Noah Cowan, artistic director of TIFF Bell Lightbox, and Sabbas Joseph, Director, Wizcraft along with members of the Kapoor family came together to pay tribute to the life and work of Indian actor, director, mogul and legend Raj Kapoor, as presented in partnership by TIFF (Toronto International Film Festival), the International Indian Film Academy (IIFA), and the Government of Ontario.[[129]](#cite_note-130) In 2011, it was announced that Kapoor will be inducted into the [Brampton Walk of Fame](/wiki/Brampton_Walk_of_Fame "Brampton Walk of Fame") in Ontario, Canada.[[130]](#cite_note-2011-06-09-Lancashire-131) From December 13 to 15, 2024, a special screening of Raj Kapoor's most famous films were held in 40 cities and 135 theaters across India for his 100th Birthday Anniversary celebration.[[131]](#cite_note-132)

### Shankar–Jaikishan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raj_Kapoor&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Shankar–Jaikishan")]

[Shankar–Jaikishan](/wiki/Shankar%E2%80%93Jaikishan "Shankar–Jaikishan") were Raj Kapoor's music directors of choice. He worked with them in 20 films in all including 10 of his own productions from *[Barsaat](/wiki/Barsaat_(1949_film) "Barsaat (1949 film)")* until *[Kal Aaj Aur Kal](/wiki/Kal_Aaj_Aur_Kal "Kal Aaj Aur Kal")*. (*[Jagte Raho](/wiki/Jagte_Raho "Jagte Raho")* with [Salil Chowdhury](/wiki/Salil_Chowdhury "Salil Chowdhury") and *[Ab Dilli Dur Nahin](/wiki/Ab_Dilli_Dur_Nahin_(1957_film) "Ab Dilli Dur Nahin (1957 film)")* with [Dattaram](/wiki/Dattaram_Wadkar "Dattaram Wadkar"), Shankar-Jaikishan's assistant music director, being two exceptions in this period). Only after Jaikishan died, did he turn to a different music director – [Laxmikant–Pyarelal](/wiki/Laxmikant%E2%80%93Pyarelal "Laxmikant–Pyarelal") for *[Bobby](/wiki/Bobby_(1973_film) "Bobby (1973 film)")*, *[Satyam Shivam Sundaram](/wiki/Satyam_Shivam_Sundaram "Satyam Shivam Sundaram")* and *[Prem Rog](/wiki/Prem_Rog "Prem Rog")* (later on, his children used Laxmikant-Pyarelal for *[Prem Granth](/wiki/Prem_Granth "Prem Granth")* as well), [Rahul Dev Burman](/wiki/R.D._Burman "R.D. Burman") for *[Dharam Karam](/wiki/Dharam_Karam "Dharam Karam")*, and [Ravindra Jain](/wiki/Ravindra_Jain "Ravindra Jain") for (*[Ram Teri Ganga Maili](/wiki/Ram_Teri_Ganga_Maili "Ram Teri Ganga Maili")* and *[Henna](/wiki/Henna_(film) "Henna (film)")*). Raj Kapoor acted in a movie with music by [Madan Mohan](/wiki/Madan_Mohan "Madan Mohan") only once (twice), i.e. *[Dhoon](/wiki/Dhoon "Dhoon")* (1953) & [Ashiana](/wiki/Ashiana_(1952_film) "Ashiana (1952 film)") (1952), which featured duet *Hum Pyaar Karenge* by [Hemant Kumar](/wiki/Hemant_Kumar "Hemant Kumar") and [Lata Mangeshkar](/wiki/Lata_Mangeshkar "Lata Mangeshkar"), the only instance of Hemant Kumar giving playback to Raj Kapoor, and did only one movie with [O. P. Nayyar](/wiki/O._P._Nayyar "O. P. Nayyar") (*[Do Ustad](/wiki/Do_Ustad_(1959) "Do Ustad (1959)")*).

### Mukesh and Manna Dey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raj_Kapoor&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Mukesh and Manna Dey")]

[Mukesh](/wiki/Mukesh_(singer) "Mukesh (singer)") was Raj Kapoor's almost exclusive singing voice in almost all of his films. Also, when Mukesh died, Raj had said, *Main ne apni aawaaz ko kho diya...* (I have lost my voice...). However [Manna Dey](/wiki/Manna_Dey "Manna Dey") has also sung many notable and super-hit songs for Raj Kapoor, for instance in *[Shree 420](/wiki/Shree_420 "Shree 420")* and *[Chori Chori](/wiki/Chori_Chori_(1956_film) "Chori Chori (1956 film)")*.
